name: Europium
name_origin: Named after the continent of Europe.

occurrence.abundance_summary: One of the rarer rare-earth elements (~2 ppm in crust); never found as the free element. Recovered chiefly from monazite and bastnaesite.

compounds.name: Europium(III) oxide
compounds.summary: Pale pink solid; doping agent giving the strong red emission used in colour CRTs and trichromatic fluorescent lamps.

compounds.name: Europium(III) chloride
compounds.summary: Pale yellow hygroscopic salt; precursor to europium phosphors and shift reagent for NMR.

compounds.name: Europium(II) sulfide
compounds.summary: Black ferromagnetic semiconductor studied for spintronic and magneto-optical applications.

compounds.name: Europium-doped yttrium oxide
compounds.summary: Red phosphor (YOX) used historically in television cathode-ray tubes and in compact fluorescent lamps.

uses: Red phosphors in colour television cathode-ray tubes and trichromatic fluorescent lamps (Y2O3:Eu3+)
Blue phosphors in compact fluorescent and white LED lighting (BaMgAl10O17:Eu2+)
Anti-counterfeiting fluorescent inks on euro banknotes
Neutron-absorbing control rods in some nuclear reactors
Scintillators and phosphors for X-ray imaging plates
Lanthanide shift reagents in NMR spectroscopy
reactivity_summary: Softest and most reactive of the lanthanides; ignites in air above ~150 degrees C and reacts vigorously with water like calcium. Has the largest body-centred-cubic unit cell of any lanthanide and shows accessible Eu(II) chemistry.
isotopes_summary: Europium has two naturally occurring isotopes: Eu-151 (~47.81%) and Eu-153 (~52.19%). Eu-151 was recently shown to be very weakly radioactive (alpha decay, half-life ~5x10^18 years). Eu-152 and Eu-154 are important reactor-produced gamma sources.

citations.source: Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Europium
IUPAC Periodic Table of the Elements (2021)
CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 102nd Edition
